# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — Forward Pass of the Shallow Seq2Seq

We derive the forward propagation for a single training example $(X, Y)$ where $X = (x_1, \dots, x_{T_x})$ and $Y = (y_1, \dots, y_{T_y})$.

**Step 1 — Source Embedding**

Each source token $x_t$ is an integer index. It is mapped to a dense vector via the embedding matrix $\mathbf{E}_{\text{src}} \in \mathbb{R}^{V_{\text{src}} \times E}$:

$$\mathbf{e}_t^{\text{src}} = \mathbf{E}_{\text{src}}[x_t] \in \mathbb{R}^{E}$$

**Step 2 — Encoder Recurrence (LSTM)**

A single LSTM cell maintains hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ and cell state $\mathbf{s}_t \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$. The gate equations are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_f \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t^{\text{src}}] + \mathbf{b}_f\right) \\
\mathbf{i}_t &= \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_i \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t^{\text{src}}] + \mathbf{b}_i\right) \\
\tilde{\mathbf{s}}_t &= \tanh\left(\mathbf{W}_s \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t^{\text{src}}] + \mathbf{b}_s\right) \\
\mathbf{s}_t &= \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{s}_{t-1} + \mathbf{i}_t \odot \tilde{\mathbf{s}}_t \\
\mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma\left(\mathbf{W}_o \cdot [\mathbf{h}_{t-1}, \mathbf{e}_t^{\text{src}}] + \mathbf{b}_o\right) \\
\mathbf{h}_t &= \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{s}_t)
\end{aligned}$$

The context vector is the final encoder hidden state:

$$\mathbf{c}_{\text{enc}} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$$

**Step 3 — Decoder Initialisation**

$$\mathbf{h}_0^{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{c}_{\text{enc}}, \quad \mathbf{s}_0^{\text{dec}} = \mathbf{s}_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$$

**Step 4 — Target Embedding & Decoder Recurrence**

For target time-step $t$, the input is the embedding of the previous ground-truth token (under teacher forcing):

$$\mathbf{e}_t^{\text{tgt}} = \mathbf{E}_{\text{tgt}}[y_{t-1}]$$

The decoder LSTM updates analogously to yield $\mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}}$.

**Step 5 — Output Projection & Softmax**

A dense layer projects the hidden state to vocabulary logits, followed by softmax:

$$\mathbf{z}_t = \mathbf{W}_v \mathbf{h}_t^{\text{dec}} + \mathbf{b}_v \in \mathbb{R}^{V_{\text{tgt}}}$$

$$P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X) = \frac{\exp(\mathbf{z}_t)}{\sum_{j=1}^{V_{\text{tgt}}} \exp(\mathbf{z}_t)_j}$$

**Step 6 — Loss Computation**

The per-token cross-entropy and batch loss:

$$\mathcal{L}_t = -\sum_{j=1}^{V_{\text{tgt}}} \mathbb{1}\{y_t = j\} \cdot \log P(y_t = j \mid y_{<t}, X) = -\log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, X)$$

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{t=1}^{T_y^{(i)}} \mathcal{L}_t^{(i)}$$

Padding tokens are excluded via a mask $M_t \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{masked}} = \frac{\sum_{t} M_t \cdot \mathcal{L}_t}{\sum_t M_t + \epsilon}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reference Table for Implementation Choices

| Decision Point | Recommended Choice | KTU Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| RNN Cell | LSTM (over Vanilla RNN / GRU) | Mitigates vanishing gradient on long sequences |
| Embedding Dim ($E$) | $64 - 128$ | Sufficient for shallow model; avoids overfitting |
| Hidden Dim ($H$) | $128 - 256$ | Balance between capacity and training time |
| Optimiser | Adam ($\eta = 0.001$) | Adaptive learning rate, robust defaults |
| Loss Function | Sparse Categorical Cross-Entropy | Integer targets, no one-hot overhead |
| Batch Size ($B$) | $32 - 64$ | Standard for translation tasks |
| Teacher Forcing Prob. | $0.5$ (anneal to 0) | Bridges train–inference mismatch |
| Max Sentence Length | $20$ tokens | Truncates outliers, keeps memory bounded |
| Vocab Cutoff | Frequency $\geq 2$ | Reduces noise, manageable vocab size |
| Inference | Greedy / Beam ($k=3$) | Beam improves quality marginally |
| Evaluation Metric | BLEU-4 | Industry standard for MT |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough (TensorFlow / Keras)

Below is the **complete, exam-ready** Python implementation structure a student should reproduce in the lab record.

**Step 1 — Import Libraries**

```python
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.text import Tokenizer
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.sequence import pad_sequences
from tensorflow.keras.models import Model
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, LSTM, Embedding, Dense
from nltk.translate.bleu_score import corpus_bleu
```

**Step 2 — Load and Clean the Dataset**

```python
df = pd.read_csv("Hindi_English_Truncated_Corpus.csv")
df = df.dropna(subset=["English", "Hindi"])
df = df[df["English"].str.len() < 200]      # crude length filter
df = df.sample(n=10000, random_state=42)    # manageable subset
```

**Step 3 — Add Sentinel Tokens and Tokenize**

```python
def preprocess(src, tgt, src_tokenizer, tgt_tokenizer, max_len):
    src_seq = src_tokenizer.texts_to_sequences(src)
    tgt_seq = tgt_tokenizer.texts_to_sequences(tgt)
    src_pad = pad_sequences(src_seq, maxlen=max_len, padding="post")
    tgt_pad = pad_sequences(tgt_seq, maxlen=max_len, padding="post")
    return src_pad, tgt_pad
```

**Step 4 — Define the Shallow Encoder**

```python
encoder_inputs = Input(shape=(max_len,))
enc_emb = Embedding(V_src, 64, mask_zero=True)(encoder_inputs)
encoder_lstm = LSTM(128, return_state=True)
_, state_h, state_c = encoder_lstm(enc_emb)
encoder_states = [state_h, state_c]
```

**Step 5 — Define the Shallow Decoder**

```python
decoder_inputs = Input(shape=(max_len - 1,))
dec_emb = Embedding(V_tgt, 64, mask_zero=True)(decoder_inputs)
decoder_lstm = LSTM(128, return_sequences=True, return_state=True)
dec_outputs, _, _ = decoder_lstm(dec_emb, initial_state=encoder_states)
decoder_dense = Dense(V_tgt, activation="softmax")
decoder_outputs = decoder_dense(dec_outputs)
```

**Step 6 — Compile and Train**

```python
model = Model([encoder_inputs, decoder_inputs], decoder_outputs)
model.compile(optimizer="adam", loss="sparse_categorical_crossentropy", metrics=["accuracy"])
model.fit([X_train, y_train[:, :-1]], y_train[:, 1:],
          batch_size=64, epochs=30, validation_split=0.1)
```

**Step 7 — Inference Models for Translation**

```python
# Encoder inference model
enc_model = Model(encoder_inputs, encoder_states)

# Decoder inference model (one-step)
dec_state_input_h = Input(shape=(128,))
dec_state_input_c = Input(shape=(128,))
dec_states_inputs = [dec_state_input_h, dec_state_input_c]
dec_emb_inf = dec_emb_layer(decoder_inputs)
dec_outputs_inf, state_h_inf, state_c_inf = decoder_lstm(dec_emb_inf, initial_state=dec_states_inputs)
dec_states = [state_h_inf, state_c_inf]
dec_outputs_inf = decoder_dense(dec_outputs_inf)
dec_model = Model([decoder_inputs] + dec_states_inputs, [dec_outputs_inf] + dec_states)
```

**Step 8 — Decode Function (Greedy)**

```python
def translate(sentence):
    seq = src_tokenizer.texts_to_sequences([sentence])
    seq = pad_sequences(seq, maxlen=max_len, padding="post")
    states = enc_model.predict(seq, verbose=0)
    target_seq = np.array([[tgt_tokenizer.word_index["<start>"]]])
    decoded = []
    for _ in range(max_len):
        out, h, c = dec_model.predict([target_seq] + states, verbose=0)
        token = np.argmax(out[0, -1, :])
        if token == tgt_tokenizer.word_index["<end>"]:
            break
        decoded.append(tgt_tokenizer.index_word.get(token, "<unk>"))
        target_seq = np.array([[token]])
        states = [h, c]
    return " ".join(decoded)
```

**Step 9 — BLEU Evaluation**

```python
references, hypotheses = [], []
for eng, hin in zip(test_eng[:200], test_hin[:200]):
    pred = translate(eng)
    references.append([hin.split()])
    hypotheses.append(pred.split())
bleu = corpus_bleu(references, hypotheses)
print(f"BLEU Score: {bleu:.4f}")
```

> [!TIP]
> **Code-to-Concept Mapping for the Record:**
> - The embedding layers are *not* part of the encoder recurrence — they are the *discrete-to-continuous* projection.
> - The encoder's `return_state=True` is what exposes the context vector.
> - During training, we feed the target shifted-right as the decoder input and shifted-left as the target output — a standard seq2seq trick to align time-steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a standard autoencoder and an encoder-decoder network used for machine translation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A standard autoencoder reconstructs the *same* input (e.g., image → image) and is used for dimensionality reduction or denoising (1 mark). An encoder-decoder for translation maps the input from one *domain* (English) to a *different* output domain (Hindi) (1 mark). The decoder in translation is autoregressive — it generates tokens conditioned on previously generated ones, whereas reconstruction autoencoders typically produce a single output vector (1 mark).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the "context vector" in a Seq2Seq model? Why is it a bottleneck?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The context vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{H}$ is the final hidden state of the encoder, summarising the *entire* source sentence into a fixed-dimensional vector (1 mark). It serves as the *only* information channel from encoder to decoder (1 mark). It is a bottleneck because all source semantics must be compressed into a single vector of finite size, which limits performance on long sentences — this limitation is what motivated the **attention mechanism** (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a shallow encoder-decoder network** for English-to-Hindi machine translation. Label the embedding, recurrent, and output layers clearly. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
- The encoder takes English tokens $x_1, \dots, x_{T_x}$ as input (1 mark).
- Each token is mapped to a 64–128-dimensional vector via an **embedding layer** (1 mark).
- A **single LSTM layer** processes the embedded sequence, updating its hidden state $\mathbf{h}_t$ and cell state $\mathbf{s}_t$ at each time-step (1 mark).
- The final encoder state $(\mathbf{h}_{T_x}, \mathbf{s}_{T_x})$ forms the **context vector** $\mathbf{c}$ (1 mark).
- The decoder LSTM is initialised with $\mathbf{c}$ and processes Hindi tokens one at a time, using **teacher forcing** during training (1 mark).
- A **softmax dense layer** projects each decoder hidden state to a probability distribution over the Hindi vocabulary (1 mark).
- A neat labelled block diagram is expected for the final mark (1 mark).

**(b)** Derive the **cross-entropy loss function** used to train the model, and explain why padding tokens must be masked. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
- The model outputs $P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c})$ for each target time-step $t$ (1 mark).
- The per-token cross-entropy is:
$$\mathcal{L}_t = -\log P(y_t \mid y_{<t}, \mathbf{c}) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- The total loss for a batch of $N$ samples is:
$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sum_{t=1}^{T_y} \mathcal{L}_t^{(i)} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- Padding tokens (id = 0) carry no semantic content but appear in the padded sequence (1 mark).
- If not masked, the network would be penalised for *not predicting padding*, biasing the gradient (1 mark).
- A binary mask $M_t \in \{0, 1\}$ zeroes out the loss at padding positions:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{masked}} = \frac{\sum_t M_t \mathcal{L}_t}{\sum_t M_t + \epsilon} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$
- The masked loss is differentiable and back-propagated via Adam (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **BLEU score** used to evaluate machine translation systems. Why is it preferred over simple accuracy? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
- BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) measures n-gram overlap between the predicted translation $\hat{Y}$ and one or more reference translations $Y^{(r)}$ (1 mark).
- Modified n-gram precision $p_n$ clips the count of each n-gram in $\hat{Y}$ to its maximum count in any reference (1 mark).
- The final score is the geometric mean of precisions weighted by $w_n = \frac{1}{N}$ (typically $N=4$):
$$\text{BLEU} = BP \cdot \exp\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n\right) \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$
- The **brevity penalty** $BP$ penalises translations shorter than the reference (1 mark).
- BLEU is preferred over accuracy because translation admits *many* valid outputs (synonymy, word reordering); accuracy of individual token matches is too rigid and would unfairly penalise correct paraphrases (2 marks).

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of a shallow encoder-decoder** for translation and how the **attention mechanism** addresses them. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**
- A shallow encoder compresses the entire source sentence into a *single* fixed-size context vector $\mathbf{c}$ (1 mark).
- For long sentences, information from early tokens is overwritten/lost in the recurrent chain (1 mark).
- The decoder must extract all relevant information from $\mathbf{c}$ at *every* generation step, which is informationally inefficient (1 mark).
- **Attention** replaces $\mathbf{c}$ with a *dynamic, weighted sum* of all encoder hidden states (1 mark):
$$\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \alpha_{t,i} \mathbf{h}_i^{\text{enc}}$$
- The attention weights $\alpha_{t,i}$ are computed via a small feed-forward network that scores the relevance of source position $i$ to target position $t$ (1 mark).
- This lets the decoder "look back" at *any* source token at *any* generation step, mitigating the bottleneck (1 mark).
- Modern Transformers extend this idea to **full self-attention**, eliminating recurrence entirely (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting to add `<start>` and `<end>` tokens** to the target sequences — the decoder has no way to know when to begin or stop generating. *(Loses 1–2 marks in implementation questions.)*
> - **Failing to invert the softmax output** — confusing `np.argmax` on the *logits* vs. the *probabilities* is fine numerically, but on the *embedding* output it is wrong.
> - **Evaluating on training data** — students often report BLEU on the training set and claim the model "works". Always report on a held-out test set.
> - **Not normalising BLEU** — the `corpus_bleu` function expects references as a list of lists of tokens; mixing raw strings is a common error.
> - **Forgetting to set `return_state=True`** in the encoder LSTM — without it, the context vector is inaccessible.
> - **Not masking the loss** — including `<pad>` tokens in cross-entropy causes the model to learn to predict padding.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must appear in your lab record's conclusion):**
> - **Architecture:** Shallow = **1-layer LSTM** encoder + **1-layer LSTM** decoder. Embedding dim 64–128, hidden dim 128–256.
> - **Context Vector:** $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{h}_{T_x}^{\text{enc}}$ — the *only* bridge between encoder and decoder in the basic version.
> - **Teacher Forcing:** Feed ground-truth previous token during training with probability $p \approx 0.5$ to stabilise gradients.
> - **Loss:** Sparse Categorical Cross-Entropy with **masking** for `<pad>` tokens.
> - **Optimiser:** Adam, $\eta = 0.001$, batch size 64.
> - **Dataset:** Use a **cleaned 5k–10k subset** of the Kaggle English–Hindi parallel corpus; add `<start>` / `<end>` sentinels.
> - **Inference:** Greedy decoding or **Beam Search** ($k = 3$); stop on `<end>` token.
> - **Evaluation:** **BLEU score** on a held-out test set (expected range: **0.05 – 0.25** for a shallow model).
> - **Key Difference from Standard Autoencoder:** Translation is *cross-domain* (English $\to$ Hindi), not *reconstruction* (English $\to$ English).
> - **Known Limitation:** The fixed context vector is a bottleneck for long sentences — **attention** / **Transformers** are the modern solution.
> - **Viva Buzzwords:** Seq2Seq, autoregressive decoding, scheduled sampling, brevity penalty, unigram/n-gram precision, teacher forcing, sequence padding, mask_zero.
